subject: ap-psychology
title: Article Analysis Question (AAQ)
stimulus:
Introduction
This study examined whether background noise type affects reading comprehension and self-reported stress in college students.
Participants
One hundred and eighty undergraduate students enrolled in an introductory psychology course at a large urban university volunteered to participate for extra credit. After removing data from 12 participants who reported hearing impairments or did not complete the full protocol, the final sample consisted of 168 participants (56% women, 44% men). Ages ranged from 17 to 26 years (mean age = 20.2, standard deviation = 1.9). The racial/ethnic composition of the final sample was: 38% White/European American, 29% Black/African American, 19% Hispanic/Latino, 9% Asian/Asian American, and 5% Other or Unreported.
Method
Upon arriving at the laboratory, participants were randomly assigned to one of three noise conditions using a random number table. All participants sat in individual soundproofed study carrels fitted with headphones and were asked to read a 1,200-word expository passage about climate policy for 20 minutes. During reading, participants heard one of the following audio tracks through their headphones:
• Quiet condition: ambient silence (background hum of HVAC at approximately 30 dB)
• Nature sounds condition: a looping recording of a forest stream and birdsong (approximately 55 dB)
• Cafeteria noise condition: a looping recording of a busy cafeteria with overlapping conversations and background music (approximately 72 dB)
Immediately after reading, participants completed a 20-item multiple-choice reading comprehension test based on the passage. Each correct item earned one point for a maximum score of 20. Participants also completed the 10-item Perceived Stress Scale (PSS-10), a validated self-report measure of current stress, with higher scores indicating greater perceived stress (range 0–40).
Results and Discussion
The researchers hypothesized that nature sounds would support comprehension and reduce stress compared to both quiet and cafeteria noise conditions. The results are summarized in the table below.

Mean Scores by Noise Condition

Measure | Quiet Condition | Nature Sounds Condition | Cafeteria Noise Condition
Reading comprehension score (out of 20) | 14.2 | 15.6 | 11.3
Perceived stress score (out of 40) | 16.8 | 13.4 | 22.7

Statistical analyses confirmed that differences among the three conditions were statistically significant for both measures. The nature sounds condition produced the highest comprehension scores and the lowest stress scores. The cafeteria noise condition produced the lowest comprehension scores and the highest stress scores. The researchers concluded that semantically meaningful noise (overlapping speech) is particularly disruptive to reading comprehension, while natural ambient sound may provide a mild arousal benefit.

parts.prompt: Identify the research method used in the study.
rubric_criteria: The response accurately identifies the research method as an experiment.
The study was an experiment because participants were randomly assigned to one of three noise conditions.
Because the researchers manipulated the independent variable (noise type) and randomly assigned participants to conditions, this is an experiment.

parts.prompt: State the operational definition of reading comprehension as used in the study.
rubric_criteria: The response states that reading comprehension was operationally defined as the participant's score on the 20-item multiple-choice test based on the climate policy passage (out of 20 points).
Reading comprehension was measured as the number of correct answers on a 20-item multiple-choice reading comprehension test.
The operational definition of reading comprehension is the score on the 20-question multiple-choice test administered immediately after reading.

parts.prompt: Describe what the data in the table indicate about the relationship between noise condition and perceived stress.
rubric_criteria: The response accurately describes that participants in the cafeteria noise condition reported the highest stress scores (22.7), while participants in the nature sounds condition reported the lowest stress scores (13.4), with the quiet condition in between (16.8).
Greater perceived stress was associated with the cafeteria noise condition, and nature sounds were associated with the least stress.

As noise became more semantically meaningful (from silence to nature sounds to cafeteria noise), self-reported stress generally increased, with cafeteria noise producing the highest mean stress score of 22.7.

parts.prompt: Identify at least one ethical guideline the researchers applied in conducting this study.
rubric_criteria: The response identifies that participants gave informed consent before the study began.
The response identifies that participation was voluntary (students volunteered for extra credit, implying they could decline).
The response identifies confidentiality or protection of participant data as an ethical principle that would apply to this study.

parts.prompt: Explain the extent to which the research findings may or may not be generalizable, using specific and relevant evidence from the study.
rubric_criteria: The response proposes a claim about generalizability that references a relevant population, supported by evidence from the study's participant description.
The findings may not be generalizable beyond college students because all participants were undergraduates from a single urban university.
The findings may be somewhat generalizable to young adults because the sample was demographically diverse across racial/ethnic groups.
The results may not apply to older adults or children because participants ranged only from age 17 to 26.
parts.scoring_notes: If students assert the study is 'partly' generalizable, their evidence must support both a way it is generalizable and a way it is not to earn the point.

parts.prompt: Explain how the concept of selective attention relates to the finding that cafeteria noise produced the lowest reading comprehension scores.
rubric_criteria: The response accurately explains that selective attention is the ability to focus on a specific stimulus while filtering out others, and that cafeteria noise (overlapping speech) competed with the reading task for limited attentional resources, making it harder for participants to selectively attend to the text.
The overlapping conversations in cafeteria noise were semantically meaningful and likely captured participants' selective attention away from the reading passage, lowering comprehension.
Selective attention was disrupted in the cafeteria noise condition because the human speech sounds were competing stimuli that drew attentional resources away from the reading task.

parts.prompt: Explain how the Yerkes-Dodson law could help explain the pattern of comprehension scores across all three noise conditions.
rubric_criteria: The response accurately explains that the Yerkes-Dodson law states performance is best at moderate levels of arousal — too little arousal (quiet) or too much arousal (cafeteria noise) produces worse performance — and that the nature sounds condition may have produced optimal arousal for reading comprehension.

The Yerkes-Dodson law predicts an inverted U-shaped relationship between arousal and performance; the quiet condition may have caused under-arousal while cafeteria noise caused over-arousal, with nature sounds hitting an optimal level.

According to the Yerkes-Dodson law, the nature sounds condition (moderate arousal) led to the highest comprehension, while excessive arousal from cafeteria noise and insufficient arousal from silence produced lower performance.
